A jump start, also known as a false start, in F1 occurs when a driver fails to start the race from the grid in accordance with the regulations. 

A jump start is detected by the FIA's data logging system and/or by the Race Control officials when a driver crosses the line on the grid before the remainder of the field, as defined by the start signal (usually the extinguishing of the starting lights). The system uses speed sensors on the cars and timing loops embedded in the track to detect if a car moves before the start signal. If a driver is found to have jumped the start, they may be subject to a penalty, which can be a drive-through penalty, a time penalty or, in more severe cases or for repeat offences, grid penalties for a future race.

The top 10 finishing positions score championship points in a standard F1 Grand Prix. 

1st: 25 points
2nd: 18 points
3rd: 15 points
4th: 12 points
5th: 10 points
6th: 8 points
7th: 6 points
8th: 4 points
9th: 2 points
10th: 1 point
One point is awarded for the fastest lap in a Grand Prix. The key eligibility condition is that the driver must be classified in the top 10 at the end of the race.

The end of the Virtual Safety Car (VSC) is signalled by a message on the timing monitors and on the drivers' LCD displays reading "VSC END". This message is shown for a minimum of 3 seconds before racing resumes.
A maximum of 2 DRS activation zones can be designated on a circuit for a single F1 race.
Yes.

No, not always. According to Article 6.5 of the FIA Formula One Sporting Regulations, if a race is stopped and cannot be resumed, points are awarded if more than two laps have been completed. The points awarded are then calculated on the basis of the number of laps completed, with the full points threshold (i.e., 25 points) only being awarded if at least 75% of the original race distance has been completed. If less than 75% but more than 2 laps are completed, half points are awarded. If less than 2 laps are completed, no points are awarded.
No.

The purpose of the formation lap before a standing-start race is to allow the drivers to leave the pit lane, form up behind the Safety Car in the order of the grid, and circulate the circuit once to get their tyres up to temperature.

The Virtual Race Control Room (VRCR) controls the pace of the field once the safety car has returned to the pits and is about to release the cars, by using the Safety Car's Speed. However the actual control is via  the "Safety Car Speed" or "SCS" shown on the Line, a digital display on the start/finish line monitor that shows a speed, usually around 40% of the fastest sector time.

An 'unsafe release' from a pit stop refers to a situation where a car is released back onto the track from a pit stop in a manner that endangers another driver. This typically occurs when a driver is released into the path of another car that is on the track and traveling at a significant speed.

An unsafe release is typically penalized with a 5-second time penalty. However, if deemed particularly egregious by the stewards, it could potentially lead to more severe penalties, including a drive-through penalty or even grid penalties for future races, depending on the specific circumstances and the discretion of the FIA stewards.

To ensure that the car's floor is not excessively worn or damaged, specifically to verify that the ride height and floor shape are not being used to gain a performance advantage, by measuring the wear on a wooden plank (skid block) fitted under the floor, which must have a minimum thickness of 3mm after the race.
